description: This figure presents SEM fractographs of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel specimens annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, and 1000 °C for 1 hour. Low-magnification images (a, c, e) with 100 μm scale bars reveal well-developed secondary cracks running parallel to the rolling direction, which caption states are associated with the banded microstructure. High-magnification images (b, d, f) with scale bars of 10 μm or 25 μm display mixed fracture modes comprising ductile fracture (dimpled rupture attributed to γ-austenite) and quasi-cleavage fracture (river patterns attributed to α-ferrite). At 800 °C, the fracture surface shows a mixture of ductile and quasi-cleavage features with κ-carbides promoting cleavage along grain boundaries. The specimen annealed at 900 °C exhibits more pronounced ductile fracture regions compared to 1000 °C, correlating with optimal tensile ductility (36.6% elongation). At 1000 °C, the ductile fracture area becomes less apparent as grain coarsening occurs, indicating that the fracture mode evolution directly reflects microstructural changes in phase fractions and grain sizes with annealing temperature.
